summary: DHH argues that humans are fundamentally bad at estimating complex software projects, so 37signals replaced estimates with fixed time appetites. Instead of asking how long something will take, they define a rough problem sketch and give teams six weeks to build a great version. The key mechanism is treating time like a stop-loss order — you cap your investment rather than chasing sunk costs. When time runs out, you cut scope or kill the project entirely, but you never ship bad software.
key_points: Replace estimates with appetites: define how much time you're willing to spend, not how long it will take
The last 10% of a project often contains another 90% of effort, making estimates systematically wrong
Sunk cost psychology — feeling like you must dig out of a hole — leads to the worst resource allocation decisions
Nothing kills team momentum like being unable to ship, and runaway projects are the main cause
Cut scope to fit the appetite, and if scope cuts would make it bad, kill the project instead
money_quotes: Humans are terrible at estimating anything complicated that involves novel attempts at problem solving.
Getting out of estimates and into appetites has been the single most important thing we have done for our software development process at 37signals.
Think of it like a stop loss.
It is so common to reach the end of an estimate, and go no, no, no, we're 90% done. We just need the last 10%. Yeah, except there's another 90% worth of effort in those last 10%.
Nothing will clog down the momentum and motivation of a software development team like not being able to ship.
This state of 'I gotta dig myself out of this hole'. Stop digging.
The thing you cut is scope, and if you have to cut too much scope, you cut the project. You don't ship bad software.
key_insight: Fix the time and flex the scope — treat project budgets like stop-loss orders rather than chasing ever-expanding estimates.
